content.text: is the process by which a vector database merges smaller segments into larger ones, removes physically deleted vectors (tombstones), and rebuilds the ANN graph over the surviving data.  It is the mechanism that converts a soft delete into a hard delete, reclaims storage space, and restores the quality of the ANN index after a period of heavy write or delete activity.  Understanding compaction is essential for predicting storage usage, maintaining recall quality, and satisfying GDPR erasure requirements.

attrs.alt: Compaction diagram: before (left) shows two sealed segments with tombstoned vectors degrading HNSW graph quality; the background compaction job (centre) merges live vectors and rebuilds the index; after (right) shows a single clean segment with 100% density, freed storage, and improved recall.

content.text: Vector databases that use soft deletes store data in immutable
-- a design borrowed from log-structured storage engines like LevelDB and Apache Lucene.  New writes go to a mutable in-memory
.  When the growing segment reaches a size threshold, it is
(made read-only) and flushed to disk.  Multiple sealed segments accumulate over time.
When a record is deleted, the database writes a

entry to the write-ahead log and marks the record as deleted in the segment's bloom filter.  The vector bytes remain on disk; the search code simply skips tombstoned entries during traversal.  This is fast but wastes storage and degrades ANN graph quality as the fraction of dead neighbours grows.
mutable, in-memory, accepts new inserts.  Sealed automatically when it exceeds the configured size (e.g. 512 MB in Milvus).
immutable, on disk.  Can accumulate tombstones from subsequent deletes.  Eligible for compaction once tombstone ratio exceeds the configured threshold.
(tombstoned vectors) / (total vectors in segment).  When this ratio exceeds the threshold (Weaviate default: 20%), the segment is a compaction candidate.
output of compaction.  Contains only live vectors, a freshly built HNSW graph, and zero tombstones.

Tombstoned vectors occupy the same byte footprint as live vectors.  A 1,536-dimensional float32 embedding is 6 KB.  A collection with 10 million records and a 30% tombstone ratio wastes 18 GB of storage -- the same as 3 million additional live records.  Compaction removes the dead bytes.

HNSW is a proximity graph: each node stores edges to its approximate nearest neighbours.  When those neighbours are deleted, the edges point to dead nodes.  During search, traversal must step around these dead ends, increasing the path length to find live neighbours.  The effect is a gradual increase in p99 query latency and a decrease in recall at fixed
.  Compaction rebuilds the HNSW graph from scratch over the surviving vectors, restoring clean neighbour edges and original recall quality.

A tombstone is a logical marker.  The vector bytes are still on disk and could potentially be recovered.  GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) requires physical removal.  Compaction is the mechanism that deletes the bytes; until it runs, the erasure is not complete.
Milvus runs two types of compaction:
merges small segments into one segment;
merges all segments in a partition, removes all tombstones, and rebuilds the index.  Both types run automatically on a configurable schedule; manual triggering is also supported.

from pymilvus import MilvusClient
import time

client = MilvusClient(uri='http://localhost:19530')
client.load_collection('articles')

# --- Check segment info before compaction ---
segments = client.get_query_segment_info('articles')
print(f'Segments: {len(segments)}')
for seg in segments[:3]:
    print(f'  id={seg.segmentID} rows={seg.numRows} state={seg.state}')

# --- Trigger manual compaction ---
compaction_id = client.compact('articles')
print(f'Compaction job ID: {compaction_id}')

# --- Wait for compaction to complete ---
from pymilvus import utility

while True:
    state = client.get_compaction_state(compaction_id)
    # state.state: 0=Undefined, 1=Executing, 2=Completed, 3=Failed
    if state.state == 2:
        print('Compaction completed')
        break
    elif state.state == 3:
        print('Compaction failed')
        break
    time.sleep(2)

# --- Verify: fewer segments after compaction ---
segments_after = client.get_query_segment_info('articles')
print(f'Segments after: {len(segments_after)} (was {len(segments)})')
Key Milvus configuration parameters that control automatic compaction behaviour (set in
Trigger compaction when tombstones exceed 20% of a segment
Minimum number of small segments to merge in one minor compaction
Target size for a sealed segment; growing segment flushes at this size
Target output size for merged binlog files during compaction

Weaviate's storage layer is built on a custom LSM tree implementation.  Compaction runs automatically when the tombstone ratio exceeds the configured threshold.  You can also trigger it manually via the REST API or monitor it through the Weaviate metrics endpoint.

import requests

WEAVIATE_URL = 'http://localhost:8080'

# --- Get shard status and tombstone count ---
resp = requests.get(f'{WEAVIATE_URL}/v1/schema/Article/shards')
shards = resp.json()
for shard in shards:
    print(f"Shard: {shard['name']}, status: {shard['status']}")

# --- Trigger manual compaction on a specific shard ---
shard_name = shards[0]['name']
resp = requests.post(
    f'{WEAVIATE_URL}/v1/schema/Article/shards/{shard_name}',
    json={'status': 'READY'},  # re-triggers compaction cycle if idle
)
print(resp.status_code)  # 200 on success

# --- Monitor via Prometheus metrics endpoint ---
# GET http://localhost:2112/metrics
# Key metric: weaviate_lsm_tombstone_count{class_name='Article'}
# When this drops to 0, compaction is complete

# Weaviate configuration (weaviate.env or YAML):
# TOMBSTONE_DELETION_INTERVAL=86400  # run compaction every 24h (seconds)
# TOMBSTONE_DELETION_CONCURRENCY=2   # parallel compaction workers
Weaviate also exposes a Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint.  The metric
shows how many tombstones are pending removal per class.  Integrate this into your observability stack to alert when tombstones build up faster than the compaction cycle can remove them.
The HNSW graph is the most expensive part of compaction.  Building an HNSW index from scratch over
is the number of edges per node.  For a segment with 5 million vectors and
= 16, the graph itself occupies 5,000,000 × 16 × 8 bytes = 640 MB of memory during construction.

During HNSW rebuild, query latency on that segment increases because the graph is unavailable.  Most vector databases handle this by constructing the new graph on a temporary segment and swapping it in atomically once construction is complete, so queries continue using the old (stale) graph during the build phase.
builds the new index in an IndexNode process separate from the query path.  Old segments continue serving queries until the new index is registered and loaded.
LSM segment compaction rebuilds the HNSW in-process.  Compaction is throttled to avoid blocking query threads.
full index rebuild is off-path if you use a hot-swap pattern: build the new index from remaining vectors, swap the reference, and drop the old index.
Three signals indicate that compaction is falling behind write/delete throughput:
If the number of tombstoned vectors grows monotonically over hours, the automatic compaction cycle cannot keep up.  Either lower the tombstone ratio threshold or trigger manual compaction.
Increasing p99 query latency without traffic growth.
Stale HNSW neighbours require extra traversal steps.  A compaction backlog is a likely cause if latency drifts up after a period of heavy deletes.
Storage usage growing faster than insert rate.
If bytes-on-disk are growing faster than the number of live vectors times their embedding size, tombstones are accumulating.

# Milvus: check storage consumption and segment health
from pymilvus import MilvusClient

client = MilvusClient(uri='http://localhost:19530')

# Count live vs total rows (approximate via query)
live_count = client.query(
    'articles',
    filter='',          # empty filter = all live records
    output_fields=['count(*)'],
)[0]['count(*)']

# Segment-level info includes state and row counts
segments = client.get_query_segment_info('articles')
total_in_segments = sum(s.numRows for s in segments)
tombstone_estimate = total_in_segments - live_count

tombstone_ratio = tombstone_estimate / total_in_segments if total_in_segments else 0
print(f'Live: {live_count:,}  Total in segments: {total_in_segments:,}')
print(f'Tombstone estimate: {tombstone_estimate:,} ({tombstone_ratio:.1%})')

if tombstone_ratio > 0.25:
    print('WARNING: High tombstone ratio -- consider triggering compaction')
    client.compact('articles')

The effect of tombstones on HNSW search latency is not immediate -- it grows gradually as dead neighbours accumulate in the graph.  Each dead neighbour forces the traversal to continue searching for a live replacement, increasing the effective
of the search.  The relationship between tombstone ratio and latency degradation depends on the graph connectivity (
) and the ratio of deletions to original insertions.
is the average number of live neighbours per node after deletes, and
is the baseline p99 latency on a freshly built index.

Practically, tombstone ratios below 10% have negligible latency impact.  Between 10-30%, latency increases by 5-20%.  Above 30%, the degradation accelerates non-linearly as disconnected graph regions emerge.
During compaction, the database temporarily holds both the old segments and the newly merged segment on disk simultaneously.  This
means you must provision extra storage headroom beyond the steady-state collection size.
A safe estimate for peak storage during compaction:
For example: a 100 GB collection with a 25% tombstone ratio peaks at 100 GB × 1.25 × 2 = 250 GB during compaction, then settles at 75 GB (only live data) once old segments are retired.

def estimate_peak_storage(
    live_vectors,
    tombstone_vectors,
    dim=1536,
    bytes_per_float=4,
):
    """
    Estimate peak disk usage during compaction.
    Returns (current_bytes, peak_bytes, post_compact_bytes).
    """
    bytes_per_vec = dim * bytes_per_float  # embedding only
    # HNSW graph adds ~M * 8 bytes per vector (for M=16: 128 bytes)
    hnsw_overhead = 16 * 8  # M=16 edges, 8 bytes per edge ID
    bytes_per_record = bytes_per_vec + hnsw_overhead

    current = (live_vectors + tombstone_vectors) * bytes_per_record
    # During compaction: old segments + new merged segment both on disk
    peak = current + live_vectors * bytes_per_record
    post = live_vectors * bytes_per_record

    return current, peak, post

current, peak, post = estimate_peak_storage(
    live_vectors=750_000,
    tombstone_vectors=250_000,
    dim=1536,
)
print(f'Current:       {current/1e9:.1f} GB')
print(f'Peak (during): {peak/1e9:.1f} GB')
print(f'Post compact:  {post/1e9:.1f} GB')

For HNSW indexes the graph structure typically adds 20-40% overhead on top of the raw vector bytes.  IVF indexes add less overhead (just the centroid assignment, a few bytes per vector) but require re-training if centroids drift after heavy deletes.
Unlike HNSW, IVF (Inverted File) indexes can tolerate a moderate tombstone fraction without significant recall degradation.  IVF search probes
cluster centroids and returns the nearest live vectors within those clusters.  Deleted vectors that land in a probed cluster are simply skipped, adding a small constant overhead per probe.
However, IVF indexes still benefit from compaction when the tombstone ratio is high (> 30%), because:
Storage reclamation is the same as for HNSW -- dead vectors still occupy disk space.

Cluster imbalance grows over time.  If deletes are concentrated in specific clusters (e.g. deleting all documents from one source), those clusters become sparse.  After compaction and retraining the centroids, cluster assignments can be rebalanced.
Scan cost within a probed cluster is proportional to cluster size, not live size.  A cluster with 1,000 entries but only 200 live records costs 5x more to scan than a compact cluster of 200.

Managing compaction in a production vector database requires balancing three competing forces: storage cost, query SLAs, and compute overhead.  The right strategy depends on your write pattern and recall requirements.
database triggers compaction automatically when tombstone ratio crosses a threshold.  Simple to operate but compaction may run during peak traffic.
schedule manual compaction during maintenance windows (e.g. nightly at 02:00 UTC).  Predictable resource consumption and no surprises during business hours.
lower the automatic threshold to 10% (catch small accumulations reactively) but also run a nightly full compaction regardless.  Best for high-delete workloads.
maintain two index copies, rebuild the inactive one nightly, and swap.  Provides a always-fresh index at the cost of 2x storage.
Configure tombstone ratio alerts (Prometheus / Datadog) at 20% warning and 30% critical.
Set compaction to run in a dedicated resource pool separate from query workloads.
Pre-provision 2x steady-state storage to handle peak write amplification during compaction.
After any large bulk delete (> 5% of collection), trigger manual compaction immediately.
Record compaction completion timestamps for GDPR erasure audit trails.
Test compaction behaviour in staging with a representative tombstone load before production.

# Production compaction health check (run as a periodic job)
from pymilvus import MilvusClient
import logging

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def compaction_health_check(collection_name, warn_ratio=0.2, crit_ratio=0.3):
    client = MilvusClient(uri='http://milvus:19530')
    client.load_collection(collection_name)

    live = client.query(collection_name, filter='', output_fields=['count(*)'])
    live_count = live[0]['count(*)']

    segments = client.get_query_segment_info(collection_name)
    total_in_seg = sum(s.numRows for s in segments)

    if total_in_seg == 0:
        return

    ratio = (total_in_seg - live_count) / total_in_seg
    log.info('Tombstone ratio for %s: %.1f%%', collection_name, ratio * 100)

    if ratio >= crit_ratio:
        log.critical('CRITICAL tombstone ratio %.1f%% -- triggering compaction', ratio * 100)
        client.compact(collection_name)
    elif ratio >= warn_ratio:
        log.warning('WARNING tombstone ratio %.1f%% -- schedule compaction', ratio * 100)

    client.release_collection(collection_name)

# Run as a cron job or Kubernetes CronJob every hour
compaction_health_check('articles')
Assuming tombstone deletion means physical erasure.
Until compaction runs, deleted vector bytes remain on disk.  For GDPR compliance, check compaction completion before marking an erasure request as fulfilled.
High tombstone ratio causes gradual ANN graph degradation.  Set an alert when tombstone ratio exceeds 20-30% in Milvus or Weaviate.

HNSW graph rebuilding is CPU-intensive.  In Milvus, compaction runs in a dedicated IndexNode process, but in Weaviate it shares resources with query threads.  Schedule manual compaction during off-peak hours.
For collections under 100,000 vectors, compaction overhead often outweighs its benefits.  Use Pinecone or Chroma (hard delete) for small collections where compaction would be unnecessary overhead.
Expecting immediate storage reduction after delete in Milvus/Weaviate.
Storage is freed only after compaction completes.  Plan your storage budget to accommodate peak tombstone accumulation.
Monitor tombstone ratio continuously via Prometheus metrics (Weaviate) or by comparing live row count versus total segment row count (Milvus).  Alert when the ratio exceeds 25%.
For GDPR erasure workflows: trigger manual compaction after bulk deletes, poll until compaction state is Completed, then record the completion timestamp as the erasure confirmation.
Schedule large bulk deletes and subsequent compaction during maintenance windows to avoid impacting query latency during HNSW rebuild.
In Milvus, lower the tombstoneRatio threshold (e.g. 0.1) for recall-sensitive workloads and raise it (e.g. 0.4) for cost-sensitive workloads where you prefer fewer compaction cycles.
For FAISS-based pipelines, schedule a nightly index rebuild rather than accumulating tombstones in IndexIDMap.  A fresh HNSW index has better recall and takes comparable time to build.
Use hard-delete databases (Pinecone, Chroma) for workloads with frequent deletes (e.g. session-scoped vectors, time-to-live patterns) to avoid compaction management entirely.
Compaction merges sealed segments, removes tombstoned vectors, and rebuilds the ANN graph.  It converts a logical (soft) delete into a physical (hard) delete.
Milvus and Weaviate use soft-delete + compaction.  Pinecone and Chroma use hard deletes and require no compaction.  FAISS requires a full index rebuild.
High tombstone ratios degrade HNSW recall by adding stale edges that dead-end during graph traversal.  Compaction restores recall to baseline.
Storage is only freed after compaction completes -- not at delete time.  Budget for peak tombstone accumulation between compaction cycles.
For GDPR erasure compliance in Milvus and Weaviate, trigger compaction explicitly after deletion and wait for completion before logging the erasure.
Monitor three signals: rising tombstone count, increasing p99 latency without traffic growth, and storage growing faster than insert rate.
